reasoning: The Federal Reserve is currently undergoing Quantitative Tightening (QT), where it allows its MBS holdings to run off the balance sheet rather than reinvesting principal repayments. Because current prepayments are far below the $35 billion monthly cap, the Fed is reinvesting zero dollars into MBS. The time series shows a massive and monotonic structural decline since QT began, dropping roughly $15 billion per month as mortgage principal is repaid. The only potential for an 'increase' is a tiny accounting artifact (e.g., the +$13 million on Oct 15), but over any multi-week period that covers an end-of-month settlement, drops of several billion dollars are practically guaranteed. A resumption of Quantitative Easing (QE) specifically targeting MBS by early 2026 is extremely unlikely. Therefore, the total dollar amount of MBS will almost certainly be lower on all resolution dates than it was on 2025-11-23. I assigned probabilities according to the 0.02 minimum bound, slightly increasing for longer horizons to account for unforeseeable systemic changes.
